mnemonics: CPU Cores are like a Restaurant Kitchen: The SCHEDULER is the head chef who assigns cooks (threads) to workstations (cores) to prepare multiple orders (processes) efficiently.
Think of Thread States like a Traffic Light: READY (Green - go to CPU), RUNNING (Yellow - currently executing), WAITING (Red - stopped for I/O).
keyPoints: The thread scheduler decides which ready thread runs next on a CPU core.
Scheduling goals often include maximizing CPU utilization and throughput while minimizing waiting time, response time, and turnaround time.
Common scheduling algorithms include First-Come, First-Served (FCFS), Shortest Job Next (SJN), Priority Scheduling, and Round-Robin (RR).
commonMistakes: Confusing processes with threads; threads are the units scheduled for execution on the CPU, while a process is a container for resources that holds one or more threads.
Assuming a preemptive scheduler (which can interrupt a running thread) is the same as a non-preemptive one (which lets threads run until they yield or finish).
